coredata.dc:title: The synthesis of carbon dots by folic acid and utilized as sustainable probe and paper sensor for Hg2+ sensing and cellular imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Hao, Yunqi
Yu, Liying
Li, Tingting
Chen, Lihua
Han, Xu
Chai, Fang
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the blue emission carbon dots (FA-CDs) are synthesized by one-pot solvothermal method by using folic acid as precursor. The FA-CDs emitted bright emission at 445 nm when excited at 360 nm with the QY of 31.2 %. The FA-CDs exhibit sensitive quenching response to Hg2+ with variable concentrations systematically, which determined FA-CDs can be employed as fluorescent probe, with a reliable linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and Hg2+ concentration, and a limit of detection (LOD) of 1.29 nM. Notably, the quenched FA-CDs can be recovered by using EDTA saturated solution with the emission comparable to initial in succession. The FA-CDs based paper sensor can be explored with similar detection performance, and it can also be restored by EDTA saturated solution. Both the restored CDs and paper sensor can be reused in the next turn for detecting Hg2+, which allowed the FA-CDs and their paper sensor can be serviced as sustainable probe for Hg2+ detection. The visual LOD of paper sensor can be determined at 0.1 μM, notably, the paper sensor can be reused at least 3 times with good performance, which is beneficial to environmental protection and saving resources. Possess excellent water solubility and non-toxic properties, the cellular imaging of FA-CDs was evaluated with excellent quality fluorescent image results. The FA-CDs provide a promising convenient fluorescent probe for multi-application in detection and imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Detection
Paper sensor
Hg2+
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Hg2+ by FA-CDs solution
Visual detection of Hg2+ by FA-CDs paper sensors
Circular utilization the FA-CDs and the paper sensors
Study on optical properties of UV and fluorescence
Reutilization of the FA-CDs and paper sensors
Detection of Hg2+ in real water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of FA-CDs, Inset: HRTEM image and size distribution. (b) FT-IR spectra of FA and FA-CDs. XPS full spectrum diagram (c), high resolution XPS spectra of (d) C1s, (e) N1s and (f) O1s of the FA-CDs. (g) UV absorption, excitation and emission of FA-CDs. (h) The fluorescence spectra with excitation wavelength of 310–390 nm. (i) 3D contour map of excitation, emission wavelength and FL Intensity.

The selectivity of FA-CDs for Hg2+ within all ions, (a) Fluorescence spectra of FA-CDs with all ions (100 μM). (b) Bar chart: responses of the fluorescence intensity ratio (I/I0) of the FA-CDs in the presence of metal ions. Photographs of the (c) fluorescent image of FA-CDs probeand (d) FA-CDs paper sensors response under UV-light. (e) The fluorescence response of the FA-CDs upon the addition of different concentration of Hg2+ (0–100 μM). (f) The linear plots of I/I0 versus the concentration of Hg2+ in the range of 0.01–1 μM. The contrast photograph of (g) the FA-CDs probe and (h) the paper sensors with all metal ions samples under UV light (λ = 365 nm).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the (I) FA-CDs, (II) FA-CDs with Hg2+, and (III) restored by addition of EDTA. The inset of corresponding solution contrast image. (b) Reversibility cycles for FA-CDs at 445 nm upon addition of Hg2+ (100 μM) and subsequent regeneration by the addition of EDTA saturated solution; dotted lines provide guide to eyes. (c) Photographs of fluorescent paper sensors for Hg2+ detection and thrice recycles after recovered by EDTA saturated solution.
The “OR” logic gate system based on FA-CDs response to Hg2+ input and fluorescence restoration by EDTA input.
(a) Fluorescence lifetime decay curve of FA-CDs and FA-CDs + Hg2+. (b) Comparison diagram of FT-IR spectra of the FA-CDs and FA-CDs + Hg2+.
Confocal microscopic images of HeLa cells incubated with FA-CDs for 3 h. (a) Bright-field and (b) fluorescent confocal microscopy images of incubated cells.

(a) Different pictures were drawn by the cotton bud that dipped in FA-CDs solution. (b) Photographs of the preparatory FA-CDs aqueous solution at different concentrations (from left to right: 0.046, 0.031, 0.028, 0.023, 0.018, 0.012, 0.006, 0.003, 0.001 g/mL). (c) The writing obtained by FA-CDs ink with three different concentrations (from top to down: 0.031, 0.028, 0.023 g/mL) and stored them for 5 months (from left to right) under UV light.
(a) Schematic illustration of the preparation and (b) the application of the FA-CDs.
Detect Hg2+ by using FA-CDs in real water samples.

head.ce:title: The synthesis of carbon dots by folic acid and utilized as sustainable probe and paper sensor for Hg2+ sensing and cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Biomaterials and Energy Storage Materials, Heilongjiang Province, Key Laboratory for Photonic and Electronic Bandgap Materials, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Normal University, Harbin, 150025, Heilongjiang Province, China

Heilongjiang Province, Key Laboratory for Photonic and Electronic Bandgap Materials
Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Key Laboratory of Biochemical Analysis, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, Shandong Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Key Laboratory of Biochemical Analysis
College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shandong Key Laboratory of Biochemical Analysis, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, Shandong Province, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Computer Science and Information Engineering, Harbin Normal University, Harbin, 150025, Heilongjiang Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Computer Science and Information Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Computer Science and Information Engineering, Harbin Normal University, Harbin, Heilongjiang 150025, Heilongjiang Province, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yunqi Hao and Liying Yu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the blue emission carbon dots (FA-CDs) are synthesized by one-pot solvothermal method by using folic acid as precursor. The FA-CDs emitted bright emission at 445 nm when excited at 360 nm with the QY of 31.2 %. The FA-CDs exhibit sensitive quenching response to Hg2+ with variable concentrations systematically, which determined FA-CDs can be employed as fluorescent probe, with a reliable linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and Hg2+ concentration, and a limit of detection (LOD) of 1.29 nM. Notably, the quenched FA-CDs can be recovered by using EDTA saturated solution with the emission comparable to initial in succession. The FA-CDs based paper sensor can be explored with similar detection performance, and it can also be restored by EDTA saturated solution. Both the restored CDs and paper sensor can be reused in the next turn for detecting Hg2+, which allowed the FA-CDs and their paper sensor can be serviced as sustainable probe for Hg2+ detection. The visual LOD of paper sensor can be determined at 0.1 μM, notably, the paper sensor can be reused at least 3 times with good performance, which is beneficial to environmental protection and saving resources. Possess excellent water solubility and non-toxic properties, the cellular imaging of FA-CDs was evaluated with excellent quality fluorescent image results. The FA-CDs provide a promising convenient fluorescent probe for multi-application in detection and imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals pollution is a matter of great concern, as its enormously increasing negative effects on the environment and human health [1]. Among them, mercury ion (Hg2+) is one of the most toxic heavy metal, because it can cause serious diseases including cancer [2]. More horrible things is it could not eliminated by biodegradation, but can transfer and accumulate in the human body through food chain, and trace of Hg2+ can damage the central nervous system [3,4]. Therefore, the World Health Organization (WHO) and US Environmental Protection Agency have limited the maximum for Hg2+ in drinking water to 30 nM [5] and 10 nM [6], respectively. It is great significance to explore a sensitive and environment friendly probe and establish a convenient and cost-effective method for monitoring Hg2+ in real water samples. The main current methods used to detect Hg2+ contained atomic fluorescence spectrometry [7], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [8], and electrochemical methods [9], all of them possessed good accuracy. However, these methods have the disadvantages of being time-consuming and requiring complex equipment and complex operation process, could not meet the need of in-situ test. Therefore, it is necessary to develop portable, rapid and easy to operate method to overcome these drawbacks, and monitor Hg2+ in environment water.

Recently, CDs exhibit numerous attractive properties, such as superior optical properties, water-solubility, good biocompatibility, excellent thermo/photo-stability, non-toxicity or low toxicity, etc. [10–13], have been explored utility in various fields, including detection [14], bio-imaging [15], anti-counterfeiting [16], drug delivery [17] and energy storage [18]. Fluorescent probe is one of the most important applications of CDs, attributing to CDs-based sensor have the advantages of good selectivity, high sensitivity and fast response speed [19]. In particular, the CDs has exhibited more advantages successfully in the application of detecting heavy metal ions and bio-molecules [20,21]. For example, He et al. synthesized CDs with high quantum yield (82.4 %) by using citric acid as precursor, and the CDs can detect Hg2+ and I- by “off–on” switch strategy [22]. And Xiao et al. explored a CDs based smartphone sensing system for on-site detection of Hg2+, Pb2+ and Cu2+ in real water samples with rapid response time and excellent performance [23].

Herein, FA-CDs with bright blue fluorescence were fabricated by simple solvothermal method using folic acid as the precursor (Scheme 1 a). The synthesized blue fluorescence emission FA-CDs for sensing Hg2+ based on the strategy of fluorescence quenching (“on–off”) was evaluated with high sensitivity and selectivity. Notably the fluorescence of FA-CDs can be restored by the EDTA saturated solution after detecting Hg2+ (Scheme 1b), which make the FA-CDs can be reused in next turn. And the sensing performance can be easily extended to the paper sensors, which can be used to detect Hg2+ with the similar read out as a portable and sustainable sensor. Otherwise, the FA-CDs are suitable for invisible ink, which suggest its potential applications in the privacy of important information. The fluorescent FA-CDs can be further employed in cellular imaging with evident bright effect due to their excellent solubility and no-toxicity.

All chemicals and materials are analytical grade. The folic acid (FA), formamide and ethylene diamine tetra acetic acid (EDTA) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). The metal salts used were purchased from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Company (Tianjin, China). All glass was rinsed thoroughly with newly prepared 3: 1 HCl-HNO3 (aqua regia) and washed drastically with Mill-Q (18.2 MΩ cm−1) water before used.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to determine the morphology and particle size of the FA-CDs operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV (JEOLFETEM-2100). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained on a JASCOFT/IR-420 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were carried out with an AXIS Ultra DLD spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using UV-2550 spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU, Japan) at the room temperature. Fluorescence spectra were recorded on Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian, USA) and a PerkinElmer LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer. The ultraviolet lamp (WFH-204b) was used to capture the fluorescent images under UV light.

The FA-CDs were synthesized by one-pot solvothermal method. Specifically, the 0.62 g of FA and 13.5 mL of formamide were mixed under stirring and transferred into Teflon-lined autoclave, reacted at 160 °C for 1 h. The resulting solution was cooled naturally to room temperature. After the purification by 0.22 μM membranes, and then was dialyzed in ultrapure water through a dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 1000 da for 24 h, the yellow FA-CDs solution was stored in 4 °C for later use.

Preparation of paper sensors was carried out by immersing the filter papers in FA-CDs solution directly. Firstly, the filter papers cut to the strips with size of 1.5 × 1 cm, then soaked them in the FA-CDs solution for 30 min, after taking by tweezers and dried, the fluorescent test paper strips were prepared. The test paper was collected for further use in detection without any further treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ by FA-CDs solution
ce:section.ce:para: For testing the sensitivity of Hg2+, equal volume mixing of diluted 1.5 times FA-CDs solution and different concentrations of Hg2+ (0, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 3, 5, 8, 10, 30, 50, 80 and 100 μM). Then the fluorescence spectra of all samples were measured and compared. For evaluating selectivity of probe, the fifteen cations (Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cr6+, Mg2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Zn2+ and Ni2+) were picked to study the effect of these ions on the fluorescence of FA-CDs. The measurements are all similar and the corresponding images under UV-light were also taken by a smartphone camera.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of Hg2+ by FA-CDs paper sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The facile test by using test paper strips was a simple process, the strips were immersed in sample to be tested for about 2–3 min and then air dried. The sensitivity of paper sensor was evaluated by dripping 20 μL of different concentrations (0.01–100 μM) of Hg2+ solutions onto the paper sensors, then compared after they dried in the air naturally. The results were obtained by comparing the initial fluorescent images of the FA-CDs paper sensor under the UV-light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Circular utilization the FA-CDs and the paper sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence of FA-CDs and paper sensor after tested Hg2+ can be recovered by EDTA saturated solution. Then the fluorescence spectra were tested and compared with the original blank sample. And the restored FA-CDs or paper sensor can be further used to test Hg2+ in next run. For restoring the used filter papers, the used filter paper was immersed in the EDTA saturated solution for 30 min, then was taken off the soaked filter paper with tweezers and dried at room temperature. The treated filter papers can be used to re-detect Hg2+ in a next run.
Support information includes specific experimental steps such as cell culture, MTT and imaging.

The morphology and size distribution of FA-CDs were characterized by TEM (Fig. 1 a). The TEM image and particle size distribution histogram indicated the as-prepared FA-CDs were spherical and well dispersed, with narrow particle size distribution between 2.8 nm and 3.2 nm and the average size of 2.9 ± 0.03 nm. The lattice spacing of FA-CDs was defined to 0.21 nm, which was well corresponding to the (100) facet of graphitic carbon (inset in Fig. 1a) [24].

The FTIR spectroscopy was measured to illustrate the composition and surface groups as shown in Fig. 1b. The FA-CDs exhibited a strong peak at around 3420 cm−1, corresponded to O-H/N-H vibrations. The sharp peaks at 1390 and 1045 cm−1 were assigned to C-N stretching and N-H wagging respectively [25]. In addition, a strong peak was observed at 1685 cm−1, indicating the presence of a C=O bending vibration [26]. And the C-H stretch vibration can be found at 2920 cm−1. The FTIR indicated that abundant groups such as –NH2, –COOH on the surface of FA-CDs.

To further determine the chemical composition and chemical bonding of FA-CDs, the XPS measurement was performed. The three primary peaks of 284.6, 400.1 and 531.4 eV appeared in the XPS spectrum of FA-CDs (Fig. 1c) belongs to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The C1s XPS high resolution spectrum of FA-CDs (Fig. 1d) showed four peaks at 284.6, 285.7, 287.3 and 288.4 eV, which can be attributed to C-C/C=C, C-N, C=O and O-C=O bonding, respectively. The N1s spectrum of the FA-CDs (Fig. 1e) exhibited three peaks at 399.2, 400.4 and 401.9 eV, which were related to C-N, pyridinie N and N-H groups, indicated the existence of amide groups [27,28]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 1f) could be divided into two peaks at 531.3 and 533.3 eV, assigned to C=O and C-OH/C-O-C bonds, respectively. The analysis results of XPS are consistent with that of FTIR, demonstrating the surface of the FA-CDs is rich in functional groups of C=O, C-N, C-OH, C-O-C, which benefit to the solubility and provide effective coordination site to seize heavy metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on optical properties of UV and fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis absorption spectra of FA-CDs were depicted in Fig. 1g. The FA-CDs showed two absorption bands centred at 275 nm and 360 nm, which could be assigned as n-π* transition of C=C bonds and π-π* transitions transition of C=O bonds [29]. The fluorescence spectra of the FA-CDs exhibit maximum emission wavelength at 445 nm (Em) at excitation of 360 nm (Ex). To further explore the FA-CDs fluorescence properties, the excitation dependence of FA-CDs was investigated by recording the emission behavior with different excitation lengths of 310–390 nm in Fig. 1h. The strongest emission wavelengths are all emerged at 445 nm without obvious shift manifested the excitation independence of FA-CDs, indicated their uniform particle size distribution and surface energy traps [30,31], which can be further confirmed by 3D fluorescence spectra (Fig. 1i). The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) was determined as 31.2 % by using quinine sulphate as reference. Hence, the outstanding optical properties of FA-CDs endow them to be a promising fluorescent probe.

To verify the photostability of the FA-CDs, the effects of pH to fluorescence intensity have been explored. The pH of FA-CDs solutions were adjusted by tuning the amount of buffer solution (potassium acid phthalate, pH = 4.08; antipyonin, pH = 9.18), then the fluorescence spectra of FA-CDs samples with different pH were recorded respectively (Fig. S1). The fluorescent intensity increased (pH from 1.0 to 6.0) then started to decrease (from 7.0 to 12.0), indicated the fluorescence intensity of FA-CDs strongly depended on the pH value. These results manifested the optimum pH value of FA-CDs can be determined at 6.0. Key factors influencing experimental parameters, such as storage time, high concentration of NaCl solution concentration and illuminating at UV irradiation, were further studied. As shown in Fig. S2, S3 and S4 the FL intensity of the FA-CDs showed slight change with the storage time, high salinity and strong light irradiation, indicated excellent photostability performance of FA-CDs, which benefits to their application in detection and cellular imaging.

The cationic solutions (including Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Zn2+ and Ni2+, 100 μM) and different concentrations of Hg2+ (from 0.01 μM to 100 μM) were used to investigate the detection capability of FA-CDs and paper sensor for Hg2+ (Fig. 2 ). As portrayed in the Fig. 2a, the fluorescence of FA-CDs was quenched by Hg2+ evidently, other samples showed negligible change on fluorescence intensity at same conditions indicated the good selectivity of FA-CDs for Hg2+. To evaluate the specificity clearly, the ratio I/I0 of all samples was compared (I0 and I represent the fluorescence intensities of FA-CDs solutions absence and presence Hg2+ respectively). Fig. 2b described the FA-CDs fluorescence intensity ratio (I/I0) in the presence of different ions (100 μM), the I/I0 values of Hg2+ are significantly slumped in comparison to other ions, indicated the FA-CDs exhibited excellent selectivity to Hg2+. The fluorescent images of the samples were depicted in Fig. 2c, the emission of FA-CDs was significantly quenched by Hg2+. As ascribed in Fig. 2d, the similar behavior of detection of FA-CDs solution can breezily be extended to the paper sensors. The blue fluorescence paper sensor was obviously quenched by exposing to Hg2+, but the other ions reserve solutions of the same volume dropped into the paper sensor without obvious change. These results indicated that the FA-CDs and the paper sensor exhibited good specificity for Hg2+.

The sensitivity of FA-CDs versus Hg2+ was evaluated successively. Fig. 2e showed the evolution of fluorescence spectra of the FA-CDs solution by increasing the concentration of Hg2+. With the increase of Hg2+ concentration (from 0.01 μM to 100 μM), the fluorescence intensity of FA-CDs at 445 nm descended gradually, indicated the dependence relationship between fluorescence intensity and concentration of Hg2+. The relationship of relative fluorescence intensity ratio (I/I0) with concentration of Hg2+ can be determined as the equation I/I0 = 0.969–0.118 [Hg2+] (R2 = 0.996). The detection limit was calculated as low as 1.29 nM according to the equation based on 3δ/k [32]. Meanwhile, the fluorescent color of these samples gradually quenched with the raising concentration of Hg2+ under UV-light (Fig. 2g) can be observed. As shown in Fig. 2h, with addition of Hg2+ concentration, the brightness of the paper sensor gradually dimmed, was consistent with the fluorescence emission spectra (Fig. 2g), manifested the faithful duplicate behavior of FA-CDs paper sensor. The lowest observable concentration of Hg2+ by naked eyes can be determined at 0.1 μM, which provided a convenient and facile detection method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reutilization of the FA-CDs and paper sensors
ce:section.ce:para: Featured the sustainable recovery of fluorescence of FA-CDs by EDTA solution, as delineated in Fig. 3 , the reversibility in the response of FA-CDs was verified during its thrice titration cycles. As depicted the emission slumped by adding Hg2+ can be restored by EDTA, which exhibited alternating quenching and enhancement processes of fluorescence in a sequence. The addition of the Hg2+ caused an obvious fluorescence quenching, and exposing EDTA saturated solution to the quenched solution, caused the recovery of the fluorescence intensity of FA-CDs (Fig. 3a). The switch process can be repeated three times with ignorable fluorescence efficiency loss, the recovery rate of fluorescence intensity can exceed 95 % (Fig. 3b). The similar fluorescence recovery behavior also occurs on paper sensors, manifested both FA-CDs and paper sensor can be used as sustainable probe by restoring the quenched probe (Fig. 3c).

To illustrate the procedure and response of this detection system, the logic gate was attempted to establish for further application. The logic gate and truth table generated by the FA-CDs detection system are presented in pictorial form in Fig. 4 . The FA-CDs yielded an “OR” gate with two input lines (Hg2+ or EDTA). Fluorescence quenching occurs in the presence of Hg2+ alone (Output = 0). When the EDTA saturated solution input, the fluorescence emissions at 445 nm was recovered (Output = 1). Thus, the process of detection of FA-CDs based sensor can be understood evidently.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the accuracy and environment tolerance of FA-CDs, the real water samples were collected and tested. In this study, lake water samples (Chagan Lake) and sea water samples (Yellow Sea) were selected for actual sample analysis. Hg2+ samples with concentrations of 10, 500 and 800 nM were studied by standard spiked addition method. Table 1 summarizes the recoveries of Hg2+ in the obtained water samples. The average recoveries of the samples were in the range of 94.2–103.3 %, indicated the feasibility of FA-CDs in the detection of Hg2+ in water samples. These results proved the FA-CDs can be employed with reliable accuracy and excellent environment tolerance.

To comprehend the performance of FA-CDs sensing Hg2+, the possible quenching mechanism of the FA-CDs induced by Hg2+ was further explored. In general, the fluorescence quenching can be resulted from dynamic quenching effect (DQE) or static quenching effect (SQE) [33]. As shown in Fig. 5 a, in the presence and absence of Hg2+, the fluorescence lifetime of FA-CDs was calculated to be 3.30 μs and 2.17 μs, respectively. The lifetime of FA-CDs was found to be reduced about 34 %, the results preliminarily indicated that the quenching of FA-CDs by Hg2+ can be attributed to a DQE process. Both the dynamic and static quenching conformed to the conventional Stern-Volmer equation.
I0/I = 1 + Ksv[Q] = 1 + Kqτ0[Q]

Ksv is calculated as 1.61 × 104 M−1, and Kq is further calculated as 4.85 × 109 M−1s−1, which is lower than 2.0 × 1010 M−1s−1, further confirmed that the quenching of FA-CDs by Hg2+ can be explained by DQE process [34].

Owing to the Hg2+ ions can easily combined to amine groups/carbonyl on the surface of FA-CDs, the mixing of Hg2+ has evidently effect on the UV–vis absorption spectra of FA-CDs. As shown in Fig. S5, with the addition of Hg2+, the absorbance intensity of the FA-CDs at 280 nm occurred slight blue shift and the absorbance peak at 350 nm emerged with a red shift accompanied strength enhancement. To estimate above conclusion the FT-IR spectra was further estimated, the obtained characteristic peak of carbonyl group (at 1658 cm−1) of FA-CDs + Hg2+ showed obviously reduced (Fig. 5b), which illustrated that the fluorescence quenching may be caused by the carbonyl interaction with Hg2+. For the mechanism of fluorescence restoration, the EDTA has stronger chelating effect and can easily capture the Hg2+ from FA-CDs + Hg2+ system, and recovered the quenched fluorescence, which allowed the sustainable detecting performance of FA-CDs and paper sensor.

The excellent optical property and photostability explored the FA-CDs utility in the cellular imaging. Firstly, the toxicity of FA-CDs should be investigated. To evaluate the cytotoxicity of FA-CDs, MTT assay was used to determine the activity of HeLa cells in the treatment of FA-CDs. As depicted in Fig. S6, the cell survival rate still exceeded 85 % even with the concentration of FA-CDs in cultured to 0.031 g/mL, which demonstrated the low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility of FA-CDs, endows their further cellular imaging application. HeLa cells incubated by FA-CDs at 37 °C for 3 h were used to monitor cellular uptake. The obvious blue radiation in the cell can be observed by naked eyes, and no obvious effect on cell morphology was found, indicated their good compatibility of co-existence. These results suggested that the FA-CDs can be applied to highly efficient biological imaging (Fig. 6 ).

In order to explore the best of the high quantum efficiency of the FA-CDs, the application prospect of FA-CDs ink in drawing and writing were further studied. The fluorescent images were obtained by using cotton buds dipped in FA-CDs solution to draw the cartoons (Fig. 7 a) and letter calligraphy (Fig. 7c) on the filter paper. Notably, strong fluorescence can be observed under ultraviolet light, while marker text cannot be observed in sunlight, which allow FA-CDs applied as invisible ink and anti-counterfeiting materials. It is well known that CDs have self-aggregation at a highly concentration [35]. Sometimes, fluorescence quenching can be affected by the formation of non-fluorescent substances that interact with ground or transient excited states [36]. Therefore, the optical properties of FA-CDs at different concentrations were evaluated to discuss the effect from the substances or ground. As shown in Fig. 7b, the concentrations of FA-CDs from left to right were 0.046, 0.031, 0.028, 0.023, 0.018, 0.012, 0.006, 0.003, 0.001 g/mL respectively. The successive dilution maybe cause the elimination of fluorescence of FA-CDs. When the concentration was 0.031 g/mL, the blue fluorescence intensity was highest in all samples, and with the dilution of the solution, the blue fluorescence decreased gradually. As shown in Fig. 7c, the fluorescent drawing at different concentrations of FA-CDs exhibited different intensity under UV-light. The concentrations of FA-CDs from top to bottom were 0.031, 0.028 and 0.023 g/mL, and all samples can remain good fluorescence at room temperature for more than five months (from left to right). These results manifested the good optical stability and storage stability of FA-CDs, which expanded their multi-function.

Facile and effective synthesis of FA-CDs was provided with high stability and excellent sensing performance for Hg2+, good cellular imaging and fluorescent ink application. Notably, the quenching fluorescence of FA-CDs can be restored to the initial state by adding EDTA saturated solution when they detected Hg2+. The results were further extended to a portable, low-cost and recyclable FA-CDs paper sensor, which was used to selectively detect Hg2+ by naked eyes. The paper sensor can be recycled to detect Hg2+ ions at least three times, which is beneficial to environmental protection and saving resources. And due to their high fluorescence quantum efficiency, “invisible” ability and low cytotoxicity, they can be used well in fluorescent invisible ink and cell imaging. The multi-functional FA-CDs bring more application possibility, and provide a reference for the expansion and application of CDs.

Yunqi Hao: Resources, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Liying Yu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Tingting Li: Validation. Lihua Chen: Writing – review & editing. Xu Han: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Fang Chai: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciated the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (22171060), Graduate Innovation Fund of Harbin Normal University (HSDSSCX2022-14), Beihai Municipal Science and Technology Bureau (202082024), and the open project of the chemistry department of Qingdao University of Science and Technology (QUSTHX202002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121865.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
